item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm loving your SCPs! Really digging these organizational pieces. It's my honor to push this from +99 to +100! (Though I guess it might have already been up then down? Who knows?)

I'm inspired to draw wacky organizational charts after this. Loved the guy being his own boss in the interview, via a… How do you say this in English… The chain was long, but the element of it that really stood out for me was a vacant office with a "Go to this other place for now" sign. It's so… dynamic how this works accounting for vacant, "just-for-now" spaces. I want to believe it's in purpose, like there's an SCP-3442 text somewhere that says "At all points, you must have one of the 20 sub-branch managers be fired; rotate accordingly".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Late reply, but I think that name's probably the best few words I've ever put together. If I remember correctly, it's actually what I built the entire article around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: YO! I first read this SCP when it first came out and I JUST NOW got the joke behind the MTF name, you cheeky son of a bitch!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was recently forced through an experience similar to the Agent's at my own place of work, so I can sympathize.

(In summary, I am 'Site Content Owner' for a specific, small thing. I had to add a new user group. I contacted IT who told me to talk to the Site Content Owner. They wouldn't accept that I was the Site Content Owner and pushed me further and further back until I had to contact the architect of our IT system via his personal e-mail address because he no longer works for the company).

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Came here to double check that I understood the punchline. "Division-16-Samekh" did seem indicitive of exactly what the SCP is about, but like you said, it's a name convention that isn't too outlandish for the Foundation, considering all of the other names they use.

As someone who works in the office of a largish company, I can certainly see some hilarity in the organizations implied. Maybe I shouldn't be reading this one from the office though…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd say it's the line "Any incident follow-up should be directed to the newly-formed Site-82 Senior Operational Management Division-16-Samekh for processing," which suggests:

  • The relevant Foundation site has been infected, as it's spawned a division with a name implying the existence a hopelessly complex org chart.
  • The wider Foundation has been infected to some degree, since there are plenty of groups with equally ridiculous names spread across other articles.

It's a pretty standard punchline in the end. There's also probably a case to be made for it being the reveal in the stage 3 description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm confused on what the punchline is, can someone explain or point it out to me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Addendum 3442-B: An Infohazardous Sunday Morning Comic

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was very close to thinking that last addendum was going to be a Dilbert strip. +1

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: For records-keeping purposes, the image source is here and thus CC-BY-SA 3.0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you ended up liking it. You telling me that the other one wasn't really working at all helped out a lot. Otherwise I'd probably still have something overly blunt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps it's because I've been reading a bit too much Charles Stross recently but I was inordinately amused by the summary of traits even though the punchline was predictable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely love the punchline.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My mistake. I was infected by SCP-3442

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is brilliance.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think my office might be infected.
Please send help.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I appreciate the offer, I think it's probably best to not bother other people for feedback on a finished piece. If there are things they want to say, I'm sure they will of their own accord. If you'd like to say something about it that you don't think is suitable for public posting for whatever reason, feel free to PM me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I came up with a few different comments that I ended up deleting. It seems that commenting is not my area of expertise. Would you like me to redirect you to someone who is good at constructive critique?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you!

I assume most people on this site suffer from the first two conditions, if not all three. It seems to me like a reasonable advisory for an SCP that could come to mind if a person extrapolated enough from the provided information. It's an idea I come back to a lot, similar to my mentions of people repeating thought processes erased by amnestics if their surroundings aren't changed as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DO NOT CONTINUE IF YOU EXPERIENCE ANY OF THE FOLLOWING:
PROPENSITY TO SPECULATE, INTENSE CURIOSITY, OR OBSESSIVE TIDINESS

Well, that's me three times over, so…

This looks a lot more polished-up! +1 for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thoroughly enjoyed this. FWIW, I spent my entire career in the United States Federal Civil Service, so some of this was quite evocative. My husband, who's been private sector his whole career, has disabused me of my erroneous assumption that private businesses are run with ruthless efficiency.
Part of what I liked was the use of a familiar part of contemporary society that doesn't get used often.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A little hard to follow due to the corporate-speak, but it ramps up pretty steadily and it's oddly kind of fun.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Petrograd for looking at my earliest draft and providing extensive feedback. Thanks to Zyn and Pardalis for helping me wrangle it into a final package.

It's more abstract than my other SCPs, which ended up being a challenge for me when it came to keeping it a reasonable length. I personally think I managed it well, but then again, I usually think I did a good job. Anyway, I look forward to hearing what everyone thinks.

I promise I'll do something less intrinsically goofy next time.
